explanation: The requirement states that any normal operating mode OR any identified failure mode must always result in the system being in a state that is safe for the patient. The atomic proposition 'patientSafe' directly corresponds to the patient being safe. To capture that the patient must always be safe, regardless of operating or failure mode, we use the LTL property 'G(patientSafe)' (Globally, patientSafe holds). This ensures that under all circumstances throughout the execution, the system will always be in a state where 'patientSafe' is true, fulfilling the requirement for continuous safety irrespective of the system's state or mode.

The requirement states that any normal operating mode or identified failure mode of the system and its components shall always result in a safe state for the patient. In LTL, this is best captured by asserting that at all times (globally), the system is either in normal operating mode or failure mode, and in such cases, the patient must always be safe. From the atomic propositions, 'PCVMode' and 'PSVMode' represent normal operating modes, and 'failSafeMode' represents an identified failure mode. 'patientSafe' indicates the system is safe for the patient. Thus, we require that always if the system is in any of these modes, then the patient must be safe. This is represented as: G( (PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode) -> patientSafe ). Template 30 is chosen for such universal conditions over all times.

The natural language requirement states that any normal operating mode or any identified failure mode of the system and its components must always result in a safe state for the patient. The best-fitting translation into LTL is to require that, at all times (globally), the patient is always in a safe state. Within the given atomic propositions, 'patientSafe' directly expresses the safety of the patient. Therefore, using the 'Globally' template ensures that under all conditions, the property 'patientSafe' holds. This matches the "shall always result in a state of the system that is safe for the patient" wording in the requirement.

The requirement states that in any normal operation or in any identified failure mode of the system or its components, the resulting state of the system must always be one in which the patient is safe. This means it must be invariant over all time that whenever the system is operating (in normal or failure mode), the patient must be safe. From the provided atomic propositions, 'patientSafe' directly encodes the property that the patient is safe. Thus, we choose a simple safety invariant, using the 'globally' operator: at all times, the system must be safe for the patient (i.e., patientSafe is always true). This is most directly captured by template index 7, 'G bool_exp3'.

The input states that any normal operating mode or identified failure mode of the system or its components must always yield a patient-safe state. The atomic proposition 'patientSafe' encapsulates this requirement as it directly indicates the safety of the patient. To address the requirement for all states and modes, the strongest safety guarantee in LTL is "globally patientSafe", meaning that at all points in time, regardless of any occurring mode or identified failure, the system remains safe for the patient. This LTL property is captured using the G (globally/always) temporal operator with the 'patientSafe' proposition.

The natural language requirement is that in any normal or failure mode, the system must always be in a state that is safe for the patient. As there is an atomic proposition 'patientSafe' which directly indicates that the patient is safe, we encode the requirement that 'patientSafe' should always hold. The most direct and top-level LTL property for this requirement is 'G patientSafe', meaning that in all states, at all times, the system is safe for the patient. This directly and fully captures the requirement by enforcing continuous system safety regardless of mode or failure.

The requirement says that in any normal or identified failure mode of the system (and its components), the state should always be safe for the patient. Scanning the atomic propositions, 'patientSafe' explicitly maps to 'patient is safe', which is the desired property. Therefore, the LTL property should encode that it is ALWAYS the case (globally) that the patientSafe condition holds, regardless of operating or failure mode. The strongest template to express this is the invariant (globally) property: G patientSafe. This captures that, at all times and in all scenarios, the system is in a safe state for the patient.

The LTL property needs to guarantee that regardless of which mode the system is in (whether normal operating mode or any identified failure mode of the system and components), the system is always safe for the patient. The atomic proposition 'patientSafe' directly expresses the requirement that the patient is safe, so the property should state that this is always the case, regardless of other conditions. Thus, we use the 'Globally' (G) operator applied to 'patientSafe'. This means: 'At all times, the patient is safe,' which is what is requested in the natural language input.

The requirement states that whatever the system's mode—whether normal or failure—the system's state must always be safe for the patient. The atomic proposition 'patientSafe' indicates the patient is safe. To model this requirement, we use the LTL template for 'always (globally), patientSafe must hold'. This ensures that at every point in time, the system is in a state where the patient is safe, aligning perfectly with the requirement that all operating and failure modes should never compromise safety.

To express the natural language: 'Any normal operating mode or identified failure mode of the system and its components shall always result in a state of the system that is safe for the patient,' we use the atomic proposition 'patientSafe' (indicates patient is safe). The requirement is that no matter what mode or failure the system is operating in, the patient must always be in a safe state. This translates to 'globally, patientSafe is always true' or G(patientSafe). This directly captures the requirement that, at all times, the system state remains safe for the patient, regardless of operating or failure mode.
